message.content:
Lots to respond to here. First, some housekeeping: we never closed the loop on approving deployment of your draft version of the autosession skill in /tmp/claude-drafts/auto-session-20260421T175747/ from last night.  My verdict: get it ou of /tmp and into Chezmoi source control on a feature branch.  Since the Chezmoi sync-state depends on the canonical Chezmoi worktree, please create a new worktree to use for the feature branch work.  This worktree should become the default location of **all** Chezmoi feature development work not taking place directly on main.  Record this preference ("use git worktree <your-new-worktree-name> for all Chezmoi feature development not ready for deployment to main") in the appropriate places after you create the worktree--session docs, CLAUDE.md (project-local and/or global), auto-memory, beads, just pick the memory sinks that are sufficient to guarantee with reasonable certainty that future sessions will be aware of this preference.  In addition, make sure to record a pointer to the Chezmoi feature-branch worktree in any beads linked to this work.

Next item: could you spool this entire conversation, including the previous prompt and your response as well as all future turns, to a file in the Chezmoi feature branch under the auto-session skill directory?  Optionally place it inside a "transcripts/", ".transcripts/", or ".claude/" subdirectory, but regardless, make sure it's committed to the feature branch so it can be checked out in the canonical Chezmoi worktree.  If you think this is a poor place to put the transcript, suggest an alternative.  The point is that I want to be able to go through past turns sequentially and ask questions as they occur to me without risking context loss through compaction.

Brief follow-up question for the previous ask: When I've asked you to "spool transcripts" in the past, I've noticed that turns prior to the spool request sometimes get paraphrased on write.  Is it possible for you to replay the conversation **exactly as it happened** up to this point, write the results to the spool file, and then continue to "tee" the conversation to the file, perhaps adding some minimal Markdown structure for readability?

content.text:
Good housekeeping. Let me answer the fidelity question first since it changes how I approach the transcript work, then execute the rest.

## Fidelity answer — honest version

**No, I can't guarantee verbatim replay from my context.** What I have access to is my working conversation buffer, which is *usually* verbatim for recent turns but has two failure modes:

1. **Compaction drift.** If compaction fires mid-session, prior turns arrive back to me as paraphrased summaries. I can't tell you with certainty that my reproduction of your first prompt in this session is byte-identical to what you typed — subjectively it feels close, but the guarantee isn't mine to make.
2. **Rendering drift.** Even without compaction, when I "write the conversation to a file" I'm serializing from my buffer, and I can silently normalize whitespace, smart-quote a straight quote, or reflow a paragraph. Small but real.

**The ground truth is a JSONL file on disk.** Claude Code persists every session to `~/.claude/projects/<project>/<session-id>/` as newline-delimited JSON records (user messages, assistant messages, tool calls, results). The SessionStart hook output this session referenced session ID `bba28423-abec-4381-a6be-b17ff77e71ac`. That file is lossless — no paraphrase, no rendering drift.

**What I'll do, which gives you both:**

1. Copy the raw JSONL file into the feature branch at a `.transcripts/` path — lossless archive.
2. Render a human-readable Markdown version of the same data, so you can read it sequentially. Flagged as "rendered view; authoritative source is the .jsonl sibling."
3. For future turns: tee each turn to a separate append-log Markdown file (reconstructed from my context), with a `<!-- appended 2026-04-22T… -->` marker on each entry. Still flagged as reconstruction.

That way when a subtle rendering question matters, you can diff against the .jsonl.

Let me execute. First, survey state.

    "id": "J121-9kp.2.17.2.2",
    "title": "Add PreCompact nudge and completion-report checklist (claude-protocol cherry-pick)",
    "description": "Two small hook/template additions from claude-protocol. Both tighten auto-session's completion + compaction discipline without requiring new infrastructure.\n\n## Source\n\nSurvey dossier: `docs/reviews/beads-tooling-survey-2026-04-22/dossier-claude-protocol.md`. claude-protocol ships PreCompact flush-to-CLAUDE.md and completion-report checklist-validation against bead descriptions.\n\n## Changes\n\n### 1. PreCompact nudge\n\n- Add a PreCompact hook: `hooks/precompact-nudge.sh`.\n- Behavior: reminds the orchestrator to flush current-state to cwd-CLAUDE.md's `## Current State` section before compaction proceeds.\n- Complements (does **not** replace) the existing post-compact SessionStart:compact rehydrate path. Defence-in-depth: rehydrate reads what nudge wrote.\n- Wired into `hooks/settings-fragment.json` under `PreCompact`.\n\n### 2. Completion-report checklist enforcement\n\n- Lands in `templates/completion-report.md` + new `scripts/lib/completion-checklist-validate.sh` helper.\n- Session coordinator's completion-report template (rendered at bead-close time) parses any `Checklist: [x] ... [ ]` block from the bead description. All boxes must be checked before allowing close, OR the unchecked items must be surfaced explicitly in the report.\n- Cheap invariant against \"said done, was 3-of-5\" — maps to CLAUDE.md's verification-before-completion standing rule.\n\n## Verify\n\n- Trigger a simulated compaction with state-to-flush; confirm PreCompact hook fires and writes to cwd-CLAUDE.md.\n- Create a test bead with a 3-item checklist, close partially, confirm the validator surfaces unchecked items and (configurably) blocks close.\n\nEstimate: ~1hr bundled — both changes share source tool, layer (hook + completion template), and failure mode they prevent.\n",

Goals (declared at session start)\n2. Work completed (what ran end-to-end)\n3. Approaches tried — with outcomes\n4. **Approaches that failed — with WHY**\n5. Test results — raw numbers, not summaries\n6. Data files / deliverables touched — with paths\n7. Decisions made — with rejected alternatives\n8. Discoveries — new pitfalls, new conventions, new references\n9. Code analysis findings (if applicable)\n10. User preferences expressed\n11. Open questions / follow-ups\n12. Dependencies revealed (hidden blockers)\n\nPlus:\n\n- `**Auto:** true` marker convention — distinguishes hook-generated artifacts from human-curated ones at a glance. Auto-session's SessionStart:compact rehydrate reads this to decide how to weight the content.\n- Two-phase write discipline — \"Phase 1 hits a minimum viable summary in isolation; Phase 2 fills gaps only after Phase 1 is self-contained.\" Prevents sparse-top-heavy or runaway exit summaries.\n\n## Verify\n\n- Render the template against a simulated session (one of the auto-session design checkpoints is a plausible source) and confirm all 12 sections behave as expected.\n- Confirm the `**Auto:** true` marker does not conflict with any existing `/import-session` (J121-9kp.7) heuristics — cross-check its description.\n\nEstimate: ~1hr.\n",

    "description": "Small aggregate of two pre-ship cherry-picks identified in the 2026-04-22 tool survey, folded into auto-session before shipping. Both sourced from peer tools whose architecture overlaps with auto-session's compaction + session-discipline pillars.\n\n## Children\n\n1. **Exit-summary upgrade** (from claude-handoff) — port the 12-item conversation-mining checklist, `**Auto:** true` marker convention, and two-phase write discipline into `templates/exit-summary.md`. ~1hr.\n\n2. **PreCompact nudge + completion-report checklist enforcement** (from claude-protocol) — PreCompact hook flushes state to cwd-CLAUDE.md's `## Current State`; completion-report validates `Checklist: [x]` block against bead description. ~1hr.\n\n## Rationale for a grouping epic (vs. peer task beads)\n\nThe cherry-picks share provenance (survey-sourced), shared timing (pre-ship), shared target (auto-session draft). Grouping them as an epic records the aggregate identity cleanly in `bd dep tree` without forcing them to depend on each other. Each child is an independently deliverable ~1hr task.\n\n## Why not a bd molecule\n\n`bd mol` operates on protos (reusable workflow templates). These two edits are one-off and not re-runnable. Building a proto + pouring a molecule for a one-off bundle would be form without function. If a reusable \"port ideas from peer tool\" pattern emerges later, `bd mol distill` can promote this epic into a proto at that point.\n\n## Shipping gate semantics\n\nNo explicit `blocks` edge on auto-session. Standard bd epic-completion semantics (child closure gates parent closure) already encode the pre-ship gate — this epic cannot close without its children closing, and auto-session cannot close without this epic closing.\n\n## Sibling link\n\n`relates-to` edge with J121-7bv.1 (bd-formula probe) — they are peer pre-ship gates from the same survey; soft link adds graph richness for future `bd dep tree` queries.\n",
    "status": "open",
    "priority": 2,

○ J121-9kp.2.17.1 · Ship /auto-session skill (final deploy + chezmoi-manage)   [● P2 · OPEN]
Owner: Claude Code · Type: task
Created: 2026-04-22 · Updated: 2026-04-22

DESCRIPTION
Pre-ship gate task — final packaging and deployment of the /auto-session skill. Claim only after all pre-ship blockers clear.

## Pre-ship checklist

- [ ] bd-formula probe decided (J121-7bv.1) — `templates/beads/` content settled
- [ ] Tier-2 cherry-picks folded in (see Tier-2 epic child of auto-session)
- [ ] Final end-to-end scaffold + launch dry-run under a disposable `~/Source/_session-sandbox/` location
- [ ] Chezmoi-manage the skill: move SKILL.md + templates + scripts under `~/.local/share/chezmoi/home/dot_claude/skills/auto-session/`; run `chezmoi apply`; verify symlinks resolve
- [ ] `chezmoi apply` on the skill lands correctly on a second machine (smoke test)
- [ ] Update CLAUDE.md references (global + J121 project) to point to the shipped path
- [ ] Close auto-session epic with a `--reason` summary pointing at the survey report, this task, and the decision record

## Why this bead exists (not just the epic)

bd enforces a directional constraint — tasks cannot block epics. The probe is a task; auto-session is an epic. Without an intermediate release task, there is no place to attach the hard `blocks` edge from the probe. This task IS the shipping gate; probe blocks it; epic cannot close until its Ship child closes.

DEPENDS ON
  → ✓ J121-7bv.1: Probe: express auto-session bead-mutation policy as bd formula + mol ● P1

COMMENTS
  2026-04-22 09:17 Claude Code
    Created by Claude Code on 2026-04-22 as a workaround for bd's directional constraint: tasks cannot block epics. This dedicated release-gate task carries the hard 'blocks' edge from J121-7bv.1 (bd-formula probe), so the probe gates shipping without needing the auto-session epic itself to carry task-originated block edges.

## Turn 3 — user <!-- ts=2026-04-22T20:55:46.064Z uuid=6caf8cec-06eb-4cea-b5bc-7926c0f4b87a -->

Let's continue with the autosession work.  I have a few feature ideas:
- I'd like to add an "interactivity lever" to the command that allows the user to specify the desired degree of interactivity for the autosession.  At the extreme end of the scale, the user should be able to specify a --headless flag to have the generated documents, tool-use permissions, etc. scoped to support a fully-autonomous coordination agent; the generated autosession init script would spawn a headless Claude Code instance in this case.
- I'd also like to expand the types of notifications offered by the hooks and supporting scripts we've added.  In particular: I had a different Claude instance help me setup phone notifications via ntfy.sh this morning, and the resulting notifications acted as a useful "remote tether" to the work I had Claude doing while I stepped away from my desk for awhile.  This was useful enough that I'd like to add hooks and supporting scripts to support adding ntfy.sh-based alerts to the autosession command.
  - **Open question:** Are ntfy.sh topics bidirectional?  In other words, can I *reply* to a notification my phone receives from the ntfy app by sending a "response notification" on the same named topic?  If so, this would allow remote Claude communication and activity--a feature I'd definitely like to enable if possible.
  - **Architectural issue:** Ideally, hooks should be *composable*, i.e., the user should be able to specify multiple notification types and have the command generate the appropriate backend hook configuration to support the specified composition.  Does Claude Code's hook configuration schema allow multiple hooks to be registered for the same event?
    - Following this line of thought: The user should be able to specify any executable, not just those in the command's scripts/ directory--those are just there for reusability, convenience, and a form of documentation-as-code (i.e., "this is what a hook script should look like").
    - For improved ergonomics, we could include a "hooks" YAML template similar to what we built for beads ceremony rules, allowing the user to configure notifiers, compaction responders (e.g., "context rehydraters", especially for fully-autonomous agents), etc., in addition to a generic hook config fallback.
      - If we go this route, I think there's an increasingly good argument for consolidating all our YAML config templates--for beads ceremony, permissions, notifications/hooks, agent interactivity level, repo preferences, etc.--into a single config template that the user can either (a) install globally, with default preferences, or (b) override on a per-autosession basis on scaffolding.  Because it's an LLM tool, we could even allow users to mix natural-language and YAML-based configs to a certain extent--for example, by providing a "hook_prompt" or "repo_prompt"
    - A possible safety mechanism would be to only allow autosessions to run hook command executables in an autogenerated scripts/ subdirectory of the session's working dir, but to have the scaffolding script create symlinks or Bash shims pointing to any user-specified scripts or executables the user specifies as hooks.  Taking this to an extreme, particularly for fully-autonomous autosessions, the user could specify a "command whitelist" and have the scaffolder create shims for every command on the whitelist, place them in the scripts/ working dir, and then simply grant the agent blanket permissions of the form "allow commands like $(pwd)/scripts/*".  Does this buy us anything, either in terms of safety or architecturally?


---

input.command:
cd ~/.local/share/chezmoi && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(claude): add render-jsonl.py for session transcript rendering

Renders Claude Code session JSONL logs into human-readable Markdown. Lossy
by design (thinking blocks omitted, tool payloads truncated) — the JSONL
itself remains the authoritative source. Idempotent --append mode uses the
per-record uuid as the merge key, so it can tee a live session without
re-rendering finalized turns.

Lands on main so any Claude session can invoke it; will back a planned
/spool skill and is the default renderer for auto-session transcripts
captured in feature branches.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log -1 --oneline

input.command:
set -e
TDIR=~/Source/chezmoi-features/home/dot_claude/skills/auto-session/.transcripts
mkdir -p "$TDIR"
SRC=~/.claude/projects/-home-hactar-Source-J121/bba28423-abec-4381-a6be-b17ff77e71ac.jsonl

# Snapshot the raw JSONL (authoritative)
cp "$SRC" "$TDIR/2026-04-22-autosession-design.jsonl"

# Render readable Markdown sibling
python3 ~/.local/share/chezmoi/home/dot_claude/scripts/executable_render-jsonl.py \
  "$TDIR/2026-04-22-autosession-design.jsonl" \
  "$TDIR/2026-04-22-autosession-design.md"

# Write a README explaining the transcripts convention
cat > "$TDIR/README.md" <<'EOF'
# `.transcripts/`

Snapshot record of design conversations that shaped this skill. Two files per session:

- `<date>-<topic>.jsonl` — **authoritative** raw Claude Code session log. Byte-for-byte what CC persisted; no rendering, no paraphrase. Diff against this if a Markdown rendering looks off.
- `<date>-<topic>.md` — lossy human-readable rendering generated by `~/.claude/scripts/render-jsonl.py`. `thinking` blocks omitted; tool payloads truncated at 1500 chars; system/snapshot/attachment records skipped.

## Why committed

These transcripts are snapshot-in-time artifacts of design intent — useful for
(a) revisiting rejected alternatives without re-deriving them, (b) auditing
which decisions were made under which context, (c) catching cases where an
implementation drifted from its design rationale. Keeping them alongside the
skill, rather than in `tasks/`, preserves the link when the skill is
re-homed or exported.

## Why `chezmoiignore`d

These files are working-author material, not skill deliverables. `chezmoi
apply` skips them so the installed `~/.claude/skills/auto-session/` stays lean.
The feature-branch worktree (and anyone checking out the branch in the
canonical worktree) still sees them.

## Regenerating

```sh
python3 ~/.claude/scripts/render-jsonl.py <session.jsonl> <out.md>           # full render
python3 ~/.claude/scripts/render-jsonl.py --append <session.jsonl> <out.md>  # incremental
```

Find the JSONL for a live session under `~/.claude/projects/<project-slug>/<session-id>.jsonl`.
EOF

ls -la "$TDIR"

input.command:
cd ~/Source/chezmoi-features && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(claude): import /auto-session skill draft from /tmp

Imports the session-scoped autonomous-coordinator skill from the 2026-04-21/22
design draft into chezmoi source control on a feature branch. Nothing is
activated on main yet — chezmoi apply is deliberately deferred until the
pre-ship checklist (bead J121-9kp.2.17.1) clears. The `.transcripts/` subdir
(added in a follow-up commit) is a leading-dot dir and so is automatically
excluded by chezmoi's source-tree conventions.

Contents:
- SKILL.md — skill definition
- install.sh — global install (agent symlink only)
- agents/session-reviewer.md — bundled review subagent
- hooks/settings-fragment.json — per-session hook template
- scripts/lib/*.sh — hook-wrapper watchdog + notify-dispatcher + nag/cancel + post-compact rehydrate
- scripts/scaffold-autosession.sh — session-dir materialiser
- templates/alerts,beads,conventions,permissions,repos + README, exit-summary, run.sh.tmpl, session-claude-md.md
- logrotate.d + systemd — opt-in hook-log rotation units

Follow-ups tracked in beads under J121-9kp.2.17.1 (final deploy) and
J121-9kp.2.17.2 (Tier-2 cherry-picks: exit-summary checklist, PreCompact nudge).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log --oneline -3

cd ~/Source/chezmoi-features && git add home/dot_claude/skills/auto-session/.transcripts/ && git status --short && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
docs(claude): snapshot auto-session design conversation transcript

Captures the 2026-04-22 design conversation that produced the /auto-session
skill for provenance. Three files:

- 2026-04-22-autosession-design.jsonl — raw Claude Code session log, the
  authoritative record. Byte-for-byte what CC persisted.
- 2026-04-22-autosession-design.md — human-readable rendering produced by
  ~/.claude/scripts/render-jsonl.py. Lossy (thinking blocks omitted, tool
  payloads truncated); diff against the .jsonl if a line looks wrong.
- README.md — dir-level convention: what .transcripts/ is, why it's
  chezmoi-ignored (leading-dot rule), how to regenerate.

The .transcripts/ dir is skipped by chezmoi's source-tree convention (names
beginning with `.` are ignored unless prefixed `dot_`), so `chezmoi apply`
leaves the installed skill lean while the feature branch carries the
full record.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log --oneline -3

content.content:
55	- **Checkpoint files**: at the end of any session that advances a roadmap item or completes significant work, create `memory/checkpoint-YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>.md` summarizing: what changed, what's next, active blockers, and any decisions made. These serve as "here's where we left off" artifacts for the next session — claude-mem observations cover mechanical activity, but gestalt narrative still benefits from explicit capture.
56	- **Track cumulative scope**: when revising a roadmap or adding work items, always state the running total vs. budget. Example: *"This revision adds 8-12 hours to Phase 3, bringing the estimated total to 218-235 hours against a 196-hour budget."* Silent scope accretion is the primary budget risk on this project.
57	- **Prefer planning conversations over planning documents**: for small-scope work (< 2 hours of implementation), use plan-mode discussion followed by immediate implementation. Do not create a separate roadmap section, spec document, or planning artifact for work that can be fully specified in a conversation. The planning conversation *is* the plan.
58	- **Clean child repo working trees before clearing context**: before `/clear` or session end, run `git status` in any child repos you modified (SmartStore_Base, SmartStore_PlugIns, etc.) and ensure all changes are committed or intentionally discarded. WSL2 case-sensitivity creates phantom untracked files (lowercase `.designer.cs` duplicating tracked `.Designer.cs`, etc.) — delete these rather than committing them.
59	- **Commit and push pre-authorized repos**: the user has explicitly authorized pushing the following repos without asking — proceed without confirmation. Before session end, ensure each has committed its changes and synced with its remote. Rationale: these repos carry state that the outer J121 coordination repo does not (either gitignored/excluded or a global dotfiles repo), so their remotes are the only persistence path across machines and account rotations.
60	    - `.beads/` (nested in J121) — issue state, `bd remember` entries, config edits. Remote: `AudensAurantius/J121-beads`.
61	    - `tasks/` (nested in J121) — task working directories, `.beads` linkage files, scripts, data, deliverables, drafts. Remote: `AudensAurantius/J121-tasks`.
62	    - `~/.local/share/chezmoi/` — dotfiles including `~/.claude/` skills, commands, agents, references, and CLAUDE.md. All four of these flow through Chezmoi and need the remote synced so other machines (and fresh account rotations) pick up the same toolset.

file.content:
- **Checkpoint files**: at the end of any session that advances a roadmap item or completes significant work, create `memory/checkpoint-YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>.md` summarizing: what changed, what's next, active blockers, and any decisions made. These serve as "here's where we left off" artifacts for the next session — claude-mem observations cover mechanical activity, but gestalt narrative still benefits from explicit capture.
- **Track cumulative scope**: when revising a roadmap or adding work items, always state the running total vs. budget. Example: *"This revision adds 8-12 hours to Phase 3, bringing the estimated total to 218-235 hours against a 196-hour budget."* Silent scope accretion is the primary budget risk on this project.
- **Prefer planning conversations over planning documents**: for small-scope work (< 2 hours of implementation), use plan-mode discussion followed by immediate implementation. Do not create a separate roadmap section, spec document, or planning artifact for work that can be fully specified in a conversation. The planning conversation *is* the plan.
- **Clean child repo working trees before clearing context**: before `/clear` or session end, run `git status` in any child repos you modified (SmartStore_Base, SmartStore_PlugIns, etc.) and ensure all changes are committed or intentionally discarded. WSL2 case-sensitivity creates phantom untracked files (lowercase `.designer.cs` duplicating tracked `.Designer.cs`, etc.) — delete these rather than committing them.
- **Commit and push pre-authorized repos**: the user has explicitly authorized pushing the following repos without asking — proceed without confirmation. Before session end, ensure each has committed its changes and synced with its remote. Rationale: these repos carry state that the outer J121 coordination repo does not (either gitignored/excluded or a global dotfiles repo), so their remotes are the only persistence path across machines and account rotations.
    - `.beads/` (nested in J121) — issue state, `bd remember` entries, config edits. Remote: `AudensAurantius/J121-beads`.
    - `tasks/` (nested in J121) — task working directories, `.beads` linkage files, scripts, data, deliverables, drafts. Remote: `AudensAurantius/J121-tasks`.
    - `~/.local/share/chezmoi/` — dotfiles including `~/.claude/` skills, commands, agents, references, and CLAUDE.md. All four of these flow through Chezmoi and need the remote synced so other machines (and fresh account rotations) pick up the same toolset.

## Coding Conventions (Summary)

input.new_string:
- `~/.local/share/chezmoi/` — dotfiles including `~/.claude/` skills, commands, agents, references, and CLAUDE.md. All four of these flow through Chezmoi and need the remote synced so other machines (and fresh account rotations) pick up the same toolset.
- **Chezmoi feature development happens in a linked worktree, not the canonical clone**: all Chezmoi work *not* landing directly on `main` happens in `~/Source/chezmoi-features/` (created via `git worktree add` from `~/.local/share/chezmoi/`). The canonical clone stays on `main` so chezmoi state commands and the SessionStart sync flow keep working while a feature is mid-flight. Rationale: one branch per worktree is a hard git constraint, and the canonical's branch is used by the live chezmoi daemon/CLI — switching it puts the live config out of sync. Direct `main` commits (small, obvious, one-off) still happen in the canonical clone; anything warranting a feature branch goes in the worktree. Switch features with `cd ~/Source/chezmoi-features && git switch <branch>` inside the worktree.

## Coding Conventions (Summary)

content.content:
Add a comment to an issue.

Shorthand for 'bd comments add <id> "text"'.

Examples:
  bd comment bd-123 "Working on this now"
  bd comment bd-123 Working on this now
  echo "comment from pipe" | bd comment bd-123 --stdin
  bd comment bd-123 --file notes.txt

Usage:
  bd comment <id> [text...] [flags]

Flags:
      --file string   Read comment text from file
  -h, --help          help for comment
      --stdin         Read comment text from stdin

Global Flags:
      --actor string              Actor name for audit trail (default: $BEADS_ACTOR, git user.name, $USER)
      --db string                 Database path (default: auto-discover .beads/*.db)
      --dolt-auto-commit string   Dolt auto-commit policy (off|on|batch). 'on': commit after each write. 'batch': defer commits to bd dolt commit; uncommitted changes persist in the working set until then. SIGTERM/SIGHUP flush pending batch commits. Default: off. Override via config key dolt.auto-commit
      --global                    Use the global shared-server database (beads_global)
      --json                      Output in JSON format
      --profile                   Generate CPU profile for performance analysis
  -q, --quiet                     Suppress non-essential output (errors only)
      --readonly                  Read-only mode: block write operations (for worker sandboxes)
      --sandbox                   Sandbox mode: disables auto-sync
  -v, --verbose                   Enable verbose/debug output

input.command:
cd ~/Source/J121 && git add CLAUDE.md && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
docs(infra): chezmoi feature development defaults to linked worktree

Adds a Session Discipline rule: all chezmoi work not landing directly on
`main` happens in `~/Source/chezmoi-features/` (linked worktree off the
canonical `~/.local/share/chezmoi/` clone). Canonical stays on `main` so
chezmoi state commands and SessionStart sync flow keep working while a
feature is mid-flight.

Rationale: one-branch-per-worktree is a hard git constraint, and the
canonical's checked-out branch is used by the live chezmoi daemon/CLI —
switching it puts the live config out of sync with what chezmoi expects.

Motivated by the /auto-session skill deployment (bead J121-9kp.2.17.1),
which is the first multi-commit chezmoi feature work to run through this
pattern. Also recorded via `bd remember` and in the ship bead's metadata
(`worktree=...`, `feature_branch=...`) for cross-session pickup.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log -1 --oneline

- `1621a1c` — design-conversation transcript → `.transcripts/` (JSONL + rendered MD + README). `.transcripts/` is auto-ignored by chezmoi's leading-dot rule, so `chezmoi apply` won't deploy it.

**J121 `.beads`**:

- Metadata on `J121-9kp.2.17.1`: `worktree=~/Source/chezmoi-features`, `feature_branch=feature/auto-session-skill` (queryable via `bd list --metadata-field`)
- Pushed to `origin/main`

## Memory sinks chosen

Three redundant paths guarantee future-session pickup:
1. **J121 project CLAUDE.md** (always loaded) — the canonical rule text, under "Session Discipline".
2. **`bd remember`** (auto-injected at `bd prime`) — the same rule as a project-technical fact, searchable via `bd memories chezmoi`.
3. **Bead metadata** on `J121-9kp.2.17.1` — the specific worktree+branch pointer for this particular work item.

Skipped auto-memory — adding a fourth would duplicate without adding resilience; the global CLAUDE.md is already covered by the bd remember entry since `bd prime` fires before any chezmoi work would start.

## On the fidelity question — as promised

The committed transcript is two files:
- `.transcripts/2026-04-22-autosession-design.jsonl` — **authoritative, byte-identical to what CC persisted**. This is the fidelity-safe version. Interesting note: looking at the JSONL, I can see you edited your "Turn 3" prompt before submitting (an earlier version had only "Open question" about ntfy bidirectionality; the final version split it into "First question / Second question"). The JSONL captures both, which is something I couldn't have reproduced from my own context.
- `.transcripts/2026-04-22-autosession-design.md` — rendered snapshot at time-of-commit. Lossy by design.

## "Tee"ing forward — my proposal

The JSONL is updated by CC as the session progresses, but **my current response isn't in it until CC finalizes the turn**. So any rendering run during a turn is always one turn behind.

Default plan unless you say otherwise:
- At the end of each future turn, I'll re-copy the live JSONL to the snapshot path and re-run `render-jsonl.py --append` to catch up.
- I won't auto-commit after every turn — too noisy. Instead I'll do a single "update transcript" commit at session end, or whenever you ask.
- If you want per-turn commits (for survive-Ctrl-C durability), say so and I'll switch.

Want me to proceed with anything else right now — open the PR, start on one of the design items from the previous turn (worktree-as-default, interactivity tiers, Apprise), or pause for your next question on the design conversation?

content.text:
Great questions — Dolt's actually a much stronger answer for this than I gave it credit for in Item 11. Walking through your workflow:

## The workflow, step by step

**Step 1 — snapshot/commit.** Native. `bd dolt commit -m "…"` (exposed through bd) or direct `dolt commit -am "…"` inside `.beads/`. Dolt commits are cheap and atomic.

**Step 2 — checkout autosession branch.** Native. `dolt checkout -b autosession/<slug>` inside `.beads/`. bd reads whatever branch is checked out, so this works transparently for bd commands running after.

**Step 3 — "full access to beads but not to Dolt branch/repo state".** This is the one non-trivial step. Three tiers of containment, each stronger than the last:

- **Permission-based (weakest but simplest).** In the session's `.claude/settings.local.json`, deny `Bash(dolt:*)`, `Bash(bd dolt*)`, `Bash(bd sync*)`, `Bash(bd jira*)`. Coordinator can use `bd update/create/close/note/remember` freely but can't touch branch/merge/remote state. Failure mode: a cleverly-phrased Bash command could route around the deny (e.g., via a shell script). Mostly policy, partially enforced.

- **Access-isolated (middle).** Launch a secondary `dolt sql-server` pinned to the autosession branch (`dolt sql-server -B autosession/<slug> --port 3307 --user sandboxed --password …`), create a SQL user with `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` on the bd tables but no grants on `dolt_branches`, `dolt_remotes`, or the `DOLT_*()` system functions. Point bd at that server via `DOLT_HOST/DOLT_PORT` or the equivalent config knob. Problem: bd is built around embedded Dolt (local DB file), not a remote server connection — I'm not sure how cleanly bd flips to a server-mode backend, and that's a non-trivial discovery cost.

- **Physically isolated (strongest).** `dolt clone file://$(readlink -f .beads) <session-dir>/.beads-sandbox` — new Dolt DB in its own location. Autosession points `--db` at the sandbox. Nothing the coordinator does can touch the canonical DB because it's not on the same filesystem path. Merge-back via `dolt remote add canonical file://…` + `dolt push` + `dolt merge` on the canonical side. **This is the Dolt equivalent of the git-clone sandbox we built for chezmoi initially — and the critique I made of that pattern for chezmoi applies less here** because Dolt's `.dolt/` is part of the DB, not a separate share-across-worktrees construct (see worktree question below).

Given the tiers: for a first implementation, I'd do **tier 1** (permission-based) and rely on the bead mutation policy to cover behavior. Revisit tier 3 if the coordinator demonstrates the ability to find routes around permissions.

**Steps 4-5 — wait + commit.** Native. Coordinator does `bd dolt commit` at session shutdown.

**Step 6 — diff.** Native. `dolt diff main autosession/<slug>` gives row-level diff (added/removed/modified rows with per-column changes) and schema-level diff. Summary form: `dolt diff --summary main autosession/<slug>`. By-table: `dolt diff main autosession/<slug> -- issues`.

**Step 7 — merge on approval.** Native. `dolt checkout main && dolt merge autosession/<slug>`. Dolt handles conflicts row-by-row with per-cell conflict resolution (`dolt conflicts resolve`).

## Diff viewers for Dolt

Honest inventory:

- **`dolt diff` CLI** — the baseline. Tabular terminal output; `--output-format=json` for programmatic consumption. Fine for review scripts, not fine for eyeballing large diffs.
- **DoltHub web UI** — the canonical "good" diff viewer. Syntax-highlighted, per-row expand/collapse, schema + data diffs, PR workflow overlaid on top. Requires pushing the DB to `dolthub.com` (or a self-hosted DoltLab instance). For a local autosession review, that's overkill — you'd be pushing private internal bead state to an external service.
- **DoltLab** — self-hosted DoltHub. Docker-based. Real overhead for a sandbox-review use case.
- **Dolt Workbench** — browser-based local SQL UI; I'm uncertain how good its diff viewer is specifically. Worth a 15-minute probe.
- **Ad-hoc**: `dolt diff --sql main autosession/<slug>` emits the patch as SQL statements. You can also `dolt diff --json` and pipe through a custom renderer. For the autosession review use case, a small Python script that renders the JSON diff as a Markdown table in the session's `decisions.md` is probably the lowest-cost option — and it keeps the review artifact in-session.

For your autosession use case, I'd lean on `dolt diff --json` + a small renderer script, with DoltHub as the escape hatch for diffs too large to eyeball in Markdown.

## Does Dolt have worktrees?

**No — not in the git sense, and the sql-server-per-branch pattern is the closest native analog.**

The git worktree trick works because git's "working tree" is a filesystem directory independent of `.git/`, and `.git/` can serve multiple working-tree directories. Dolt's working state isn't a separate filesystem directory — the "working set" is internal DB state, scoped to whichever branch is currently checked out *within the single `.dolt/` store*. You cannot have two filesystem locations both reading from one `.dolt/` with different branches concurrently checked out. There's no `dolt worktree add` primitive.

What Dolt **does** support that looks similar but isn't:
- **`dolt sql-server` with different branches per connection** — each SQL connection can `USE` a different branch (`CALL DOLT_CHECKOUT('branch-x')` on the session), so two processes can operate on different branches simultaneously against the same `.dolt/`. Logically this is "worktree-equivalent" for SQL-speaking consumers, but it requires the server daemon model and doesn't help CLI tools that assume embedded `.dolt/` access.
- **`dolt clone` to a separate path** — true physical isolation, fresh `.dolt/`. Equivalent to `git clone`, NOT `git worktree`. Sync back via `dolt push`/`dolt pull` with remotes. Higher cost (duplicated storage, sync dance), maximum isolation.

So: **the chezmoi pattern doesn't port to Dolt.** For bd specifically, if you want workflow-level isolation, the realistic paths are (a) branches + permission-deny (no filesystem isolation), or (b) `dolt clone` to a session-scoped DB (full isolation, `dolt push` back to canonical at merge time).

## Revised take on Item 11

Knowing all this, I'd update my earlier "YAGNI" position to **"YAGNI only for tier 1 — the branch + permission-deny pattern is cheap enough to ship."** Specifically:

- The **branching mechanism** is basically free — `bd dolt commit` + `dolt checkout -b` at session start costs nothing.
- **Merge discipline** (step 7, human review before merge) is the genuinely new capability Dolt gives you that the YAML mutation policy can't: *the user gets to see exactly which rows the coordinator touched before committing them to main.* That's a real win — the YAML policy tells the coordinator how to behave; Dolt branching lets you verify it actually did.
- The **isolation weakness** (a coordinator that really wants to can still `dolt checkout main`) is the same weakness any permission-based system has. Acceptable at tier 1.

So: ship a `--dolt-sandbox` flag on the scaffolder that's *off by default* but available. When on: session start runs `dolt commit` + `dolt checkout -b autosession/<slug>`, session end leaves the branch uncommitted-to-main for user review, `/import-session` exposes `dolt diff` output in the approval gate. That's a genuinely useful feature addition, maybe 2-3 hours of work.

The full physical-sandbox version (tier 3) stays YAGNI until the branch+deny version proves insufficient.

---

Worth filing a bead for the scoped version? It fits cleanly into the J121-9kp.2.17 epic as a sibling to the Tier-2 cherry-picks.

content.content:
--deps strings            Dependencies in format 'type:id' or 'id' (e.g., 'discovered-from:bd-20,blocks:bd-15' or 'bd-20')
      --event-actor string      Entity URI who caused this event (requires --type=event)
      --event-category string   Event category (e.g., patrol.muted, agent.started) (requires --type=event)
      --event-payload string    Event-specific JSON data (requires --type=event)
      --event-target string     Entity URI or bead ID affected (requires --type=event)
  -l, --labels strings          Labels (comma-separated)
      --mol-type string         Molecule type: swarm (multi-agent), patrol (recurring ops), work (default)
      --no-inherit-labels       Don't inherit labels from parent issue
      --parent string           Parent issue ID for hierarchical child (e.g., 'bd-a3f8e9')
  -p, --priority string         Priority (0-4 or P0-P4, 0=highest) (default "2")
  -t, --type string             Issue type (bug|feature|task|epic|chore|decision); custom types require types.custom config; aliases: enhancement/feat→feature, dec/adr→decision (default "task")
      --validate                Validate description contains required sections for issue type
      --waits-for-gate string   Gate type: all-children (wait for all) or any-children (wait for first) (default "all-children")
      --wisp-type string        Wisp type for TTL-based compaction: heartbeat, ping, patrol, gc_report, recovery, error, escalation
      --actor string              Actor name for audit trail (default: $BEADS_ACTOR, git user.name, $USER)
      --readonly                  Read-only mode: block write operations (for worker sandboxes)
---DEP TYPES---
Add a dependency between two issues.

The depends-on-id can be provided as:
  - A positional argument: bd dep add issue-123 issue-456
  - A flag: bd dep add issue-123 --blocked-by issue-456
  - A flag: bd dep add issue-123 --depends-on issue-456

The --blocked-by and --depends-on flags are aliases and both mean "issue-123
depends on (is blocked by) the specified issue."

The depends-on-id can be:
  - A local issue ID (e.g., bd-xyz)
  - An external reference: external:<project>:<capability>

External references are stored as-is and resolved at query time using
the external_projects config. They block the issue until the capability
is "shipped" in the target project.

Examples:
  bd dep add bd-42 bd-41                              # Positional args
  bd dep add bd-42 --blocked-by bd-41                 # Flag syntax (same effect)
  bd dep add bd-42 --depends-on bd-41                 # Alias (same effect)
  bd dep add gt-xyz external:beads:mol-run-assignee   # Cross-project dependency

input.content:
Ship the first and simplest tier of Dolt-backed auto-session beads sandboxing. Gives the user a pre-merge review window on whatever the coordinator did to bead state during a session, at low implementation cost.

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation (transcript at `~/Source/chezmoi-features/home/dot_claude/skills/auto-session/.transcripts/2026-04-22-autosession-design.jsonl`, turn-pair on the Dolt-as-sandbox follow-up). The existing bead mutation policy (`templates/beads/default.yaml`) constrains *how* the coordinator should mutate beads; Dolt branching adds the independent ability to *verify it did*. Those are orthogonal — the policy is prescriptive, the diff is descriptive.

## Scope

1. `--dolt-sandbox` flag on `scripts/scaffold-autosession.sh` (off by default).
2. When on, at session start:
   - `bd dolt commit -am "pre-autosession snapshot [<slug>]"` in `.beads/`
   - `dolt checkout -b autosession/<slug>` (bd uses the checked-out branch transparently)
3. Merge session-scoped permissions additions:
   - Deny `Bash(dolt:*)`, `Bash(bd dolt*)`, `Bash(bd sync*)`, `Bash(bd jira*)` — coordinator can mutate beads but not branches/remotes.
4. At session shutdown:
   - `bd dolt commit -am "end autosession [<slug>]"` on the autosession branch.
   - Leave the branch uncommitted-to-main for human review.
   - Emit a `dolt diff --json main autosession/<slug>` render to `<session-dir>/beads-diff.md` (small custom renderer; JSON → Markdown table grouped by table+row-action).
5. `/import-session` approval gate surfaces the diff alongside the existing exit-summary.
6. If approved: `dolt checkout main && dolt merge autosession/<slug>` inside `.beads/`; branch deleted. If rejected: branch retained for further review; `/import-session` offers a "discard branch" option that deletes it without merging.

## Out of scope (sibling beads)

- Physical isolation via `dolt clone` — sibling **Tier 3** bead, deferred until/unless Tier 1 proves insufficient.
- `dolt sql-server` + branch-pinned SQL user — sibling **research** bead; depends on bd supporting a remote-server backend, which is currently unknown.

## Verify

- Start a disposable `~/Source/_session-sandbox/` auto-session with `--dolt-sandbox`; confirm branch created, coordinator cannot run `dolt branch -D main`, beads mutations land on the autosession branch.
- Confirm `dolt diff main autosession/<slug>` at shutdown shows exactly the coordinator's changes and nothing else.
- Confirm `/import-session` can both merge and discard the branch.

## Pitfalls (already known)

- Permission-deny is tier-1 containment, not a hard sandbox. A coordinator willing to route through a shell script could still reach `dolt`. Policy-enforced, not mechanically enforced. Documented in the `--dolt-sandbox` flag's help text, not quietly glossed.
- `dolt diff` on a large schema can produce a lot of output. The renderer should group-by-table and cap rows-per-group before linking to the full JSON file.

## Estimate

~2-3 hours. Most of the work is the diff renderer; the branch + permission plumbing is thin.

content.content: File created successfully at: /tmp/bd-tier1-desc.md

input.content:
Physical-isolation variant of Dolt-backed beads sandboxing. Deferred — ship the Tier 1 (permission-deny) sibling first, and only return here if Tier 1 demonstrates insufficient containment in practice.

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation. Three tiers were identified:

- **Tier 1** (sibling, active): branch + permission-deny. Cheap, policy-enforced. Fine until a coordinator demonstrates ability to circumvent.
- **Tier 2** (sibling, research): `dolt sql-server` + scoped SQL user. Depends on bd supporting remote-server backend; needs discovery.
- **Tier 3** (this bead): physical DB isolation via `dolt clone` to a session-scoped path.

Tier 3 is the strongest containment — the coordinator's `.beads/` points at a completely separate on-disk Dolt DB; nothing it does can reach the canonical DB because they share no filesystem path. Merge-back is a `dolt remote add canonical file://<canonical-path>` + `dolt push` from the sandbox, then `dolt merge autosession/<slug>` on the canonical side.

## Why deferred

Three reasons to defer:

1. **Tier 1 is probably enough.** The bead mutation policy already constrains coordinator behavior prescriptively. The incremental value of physical isolation over permission-deny is only realized if the coordinator proves to be an adversarial actor — which it isn't; it's a model following its brief.
2. **Sync complexity.** Two Dolt DBs must be kept coherent, and the `bd sync`/export workflow would need awareness of the sandbox path. Tier 1 uses the native branch mechanism and requires no sync plumbing.
3. **Storage cost.** `dolt clone` duplicates the full DB history per session. For a bead DB with thousands of issues and long history, that's non-trivial per-session overhead. Tier 1 is storage-free (branches are cheap).

## Scope if undeferred

1. `--dolt-sandbox=physical` variant on `scripts/scaffold-autosession.sh`.
2. At session start: `dolt clone file://$(readlink -f .beads) <session-dir>/.beads-sandbox`.
3. Session's bd commands target `--db <session-dir>/.beads-sandbox/<db-name>.db`.
4. At session shutdown: `dolt remote add canonical file://<canonical>` + `dolt push canonical autosession/<slug>` from the sandbox.
5. On canonical: `dolt fetch` + `dolt merge autosession/<slug>` during `/import-session` approval.

## Trigger conditions for undeferral

Return to this bead if any of:

- A session demonstrates ability to bypass Tier 1 permission-deny (e.g., coordinator finds a way to invoke dolt via a subprocess the deny doesn't cover).
- Multiple concurrent auto-sessions need non-interfering bead DBs (Tier 1 branches *do* interfere on `.beads/HEAD`; Tier 3 DBs don't share state).
- Audit/compliance posture requires demonstrable physical isolation rather than policy-based containment.

## Estimate if undeferred

~6-8 hours. Clone + sync plumbing, `--db` routing discipline across all bd invocations in the session, merge-back flow, and additional `/import-session` UX for the "discard the clone" path.

## Status

**Deferred indefinitely** — see `--defer` field on this bead. Surface via `bd list --deferred` when evaluating whether to undefer.

content.content: File created successfully at: /tmp/bd-tier3-desc.md

input.content:
Investigation bead: establish whether `dolt sql-server` with branch-pinning + scoped SQL grants can provide a Tier 2 beads sandbox — stronger than Tier 1 (permission-deny) without the duplicated-storage cost of Tier 3 (physical clone).

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation (sibling beads: Tier 1 active, Tier 3 deferred). The Tier 2 idea:

- Launch `dolt sql-server -B autosession/<slug> --port 3307` on the `.beads/` Dolt DB — server pinned to the autosession branch.
- Create a SQL user (`sandboxed`) with `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` on bd's data tables, but no grants on `dolt_branches`, `dolt_remotes`, or `DOLT_*()` system functions.
- Point bd at the server via whatever connection config bd supports, rather than embedded `.dolt/`.
- Coordinator has full bead mutation access but mechanically cannot switch branches or touch remote config.

The hinge question: **can bd flip from embedded-Dolt mode to remote-server mode cleanly?**

## Unknowns to resolve

1. **Backend flip.** Does bd expose connection config for a Dolt sql-server (host/port/user/password)? If not, is the patch to add it small (bd's Dolt layer is reportedly thin) or structural?
2. **System-function containment.** Do `DOLT_*()` functions respect SQL user grants? Specifically: can a user with `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` but no `EXECUTE` on `DOLT_CHECKOUT()` still query data from the pinned branch? Need to test — Dolt's system-function privilege model isn't identical to vanilla MySQL's.
3. **Branch pinning mechanics.** What exactly does `-B <branch>` do on `dolt sql-server` — lock the server to that branch, or just default new connections? Can a SQL user with `USE` privilege still switch branches via `CALL DOLT_CHECKOUT(...)` even on a pinned server?
4. **bd sync interaction.** bd's `issues.jsonl` export assumes embedded DB access. Does the export pathway still work against a sql-server backend, or would it need to be bypassed during sandbox sessions?
5. **Storage/startup cost.** sql-server per autosession is a daemon process; evaluate whether the startup latency and resource overhead make Tier 2 worth it compared to Tier 1's zero-process-overhead or Tier 3's duplicated-storage cost.

## Proposed probe

One-shot exploratory session — no production wiring:

1. Stand up a `dolt sql-server` on `.beads/` (throwaway scratch clone first; do not point at the canonical).
2. Create a scoped SQL user with the hypothesised grant set.
3. Attempt common bd operations against the server (manual SQL; bd-CLI flip may not be available yet).
4. Attempt branch-mutating operations as the scoped user to confirm they fail.
5. Document findings in a review doc: which unknowns are now resolved, which are still open, and whether Tier 2 is viable at all.

## Outputs

- `docs/reviews/dolt-sql-server-autosession-sandbox-<date>.md` — findings doc with yes/no/qualified verdict on Tier 2 viability.
- Updates to this bead: promote to implementation task if Tier 2 is viable; close as `--reason="not viable"` otherwise, with the Tier 3 sibling as the fallback.

## Why separate from the Tier 1 and Tier 3 beads

Tier 2 is a research question with a binary outcome — not an implementation task. Filing it as a peer bead (rather than a TODO inside Tier 1) prevents it from getting lost, gives it its own graph edges (`discovered-from` J121-9kp.2.17.1, `relates-to` the two sibling tiers), and makes the "I looked, here's what I found" output a first-class deliverable.

## Estimate

~2-4 hours for the probe + findings doc. Implementation work (if Tier 2 is viable) would be a separate bead filed off this one's findings.

content.content: File created successfully at: /tmp/bd-research-desc.md

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The conversation spans three connected tasks:
   
   **(a) Auto-session skill design discussion (Turn 1):** User requested thorough review of five design topics for the `/auto-session` skill: interactivity lever (--headless flag), ntfy.sh notifications (alternatives, bidirectionality, composable hooks, unified YAML config, scripts/ whitelist safety), git worktree vs clone sandbox, logging/wrapper framework (audit permissions, wrapping all tool calls, wrapping Claude itself, user-specified logging, beads sandbox). Explicit instruction: "take your time and review each idea and question thoroughly... Request clarification on any part of this prompt if you need it."
   
   **(b) Housekeeping + deployment (Turn 2):** "get it out of /tmp and into Chezmoi source control on a feature branch" — create a git worktree (which becomes the default location for ALL Chezmoi feature development not on main), record worktree preference in memory sinks, add worktree pointer to linked beads. Also spool entire conversation to feature branch for compaction resilience. User asked whether transcript can be byte-verbatim reproduced.
   
   **(c) Dolt follow-up + bead creation (Turns 5-6):** Follow-up on Item 11 (beads sandbox) asking whether Dolt can enable git-like workflow (snapshot → branch → autosession → commit → diff → merge), side questions on Dolt diff viewers and whether Dolt has worktrees. Then: create beads for Tier 1 (ship now) and Tier 3 (deferred) implementations, plus a research bead for Tier 2, with `discovered-by` edge from ship bead to research bead and `relates-to` between siblings. Must use `bd --actor` flag.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Auto-session skill: session-scoped autonomous coordinator with hook wrapping, bead mutation policy, compaction resilience
   - `git worktree` as lightweight alternative to `git clone` for feature branches (shared `.git/`, separate working tree)
   - Chezmoi dotfile manager: `home/dot_claude/`, leading-dot files auto-ignored by chezmoi source-tree convention
   - Claude Code session JSONL ground-truth logs at `~/.claude/projects/<slug>/<session-id>.jsonl`
   - CC hook schema: Notification, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, SessionStart:compact; supports multiple handlers per event
   - CC permission modes: allow | deny | ask | bypassPermissions
   - Headless Claude: `claude -p "..."` with `--output-format stream-json`
   - Apprise: meta-dispatcher library for notification backends (ntfy, Pushover, Telegram, etc.)
   - Dolt: MySQL-compatible versioned database; branches, merges, diffs are native; no true worktree primitive
   - bd (beads): Dolt-backed issue tracker; dep types include `discovered-from`, `relates-to`, `blocks`, `supersedes`; deferral via `--defer <date>` not a status
   - bd metadata (cardinality-1 key=value) vs labels (cardinality-N taxonomy) vs notes (append-only audit trail)

3. Files and Code Sections:
   
   - **`/home/hactar/Source/J121/CLAUDE.md`** (modified)
      - Added Session Discipline rule about chezmoi worktree preference
      - Edit at line 62 → 63:
        ```
        - **Chezmoi feature development happens in a linked worktree, not the canonical clone**: all Chezmoi work *not* landing directly on `main` happens in `~/Source/chezmoi-features/` (created via `git worktree add` from `~/.local/share/chezmoi/`). The canonical clone stays on `main` so chezmoi state commands and the SessionStart sync flow keep working while a feature is mid-flight. Rationale: one branch per worktree is a hard git constraint, and the canonical's branch is used by the live chezmoi daemon/CLI — switching it puts the live config out of sync. Direct `main` commits (small, obvious, one-off) still happen in the canonical clone; anything warranting a feature branch goes in the worktree. Switch features with `cd ~/Source/chezmoi-features && git switch <branch>` inside the worktree.
        ```
      - Committed as `ba3f7a1` on J121 main
   
   - **`/home/hactar/.local/share/chezmoi/home/dot_claude/scripts/executable_render-jsonl.py`** (new file on chezmoi main)
      - Renders Claude Code session JSONL to human-readable Markdown
      - Idempotent `--append` mode using per-record uuid as merge key
      - Key functions: `block_text(block)`, `message_text(msg)`, `render_record(rec, turn_idx)`, `render_full(src)`, `render_append(src, dst)`, `existing_uuids(md_path)` using regex `<!--\s*uuid=([0-9a-f-]{36})\s*-->`
      - Tool calls summarised with 1500-char truncation, thinking blocks omitted
      - Committed as `9f8c318` on chezmoi main
      - Rationale (user directive): global availability for all Claude sessions; will back planned `/spool` skill
   
   - **`/home/hactar/Source/chezmoi-features/home/dot_claude/skills/auto-session/`** (new dir, feature branch)
      - Full copy of `/tmp/claude-drafts/auto-session-20260421T175747/` contents (22 files, 2490 insertions)
      - Structure: SKILL.md, install.sh, agents/session-reviewer.md, hooks/settings-fragment.json, scripts/lib/*.sh (hook-wrapper, nag-repeater, notify-dispatcher, post-compact-rehydrate, preuse-cancel-nag), scripts/scaffold-autosession.sh, templates/{alerts,beads,conventions,permissions,repos}/, templates/{run.sh.tmpl,exit-summary.md,session-claude-md.md,README.md}, logrotate.d/, systemd/
      - Committed as `5f10541` on feature/auto-session-skill
   
   - **`/home/hactar/Source/chezmoi-features/home/dot_claude/skills/auto-session/.transcripts/`** (new dir)
      - `2026-04-22-autosession-design.jsonl` — byte-identical copy of live session JSONL (487968 bytes, authoritative)

      - `README.md` — explains convention, chezmoi-ignore via leading-dot, regeneration commands
      - Committed as `1621a1c`
      - Auto-excluded from chezmoi apply by leading-dot convention
   
   - **`/tmp/bd-tier1-desc.md`** (new) — Tier 1 bead description
   - **`/tmp/bd-tier3-desc.md`** (new) — Tier 3 bead description (deferred)
   - **`/tmp/bd-research-desc.md`** (new) — Research bead description for Tier 2 sql-server investigation

     - `J121-9kp.2.17.4` — "Auto-session: Dolt-clone beads sandbox (Tier 3 — physical isolation)" — deferred until 2099-12-31

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **Renderer script initially placed in feature branch** — User rejected: "put the script in a Chezmoi-managed 'global Claude scripts' directory at ~/.claude/scripts/. Make this change directly on `main` in the canonical Chezmoi repo and commit when done." Fix: moved to `~/.local/share/chezmoi/home/dot_claude/scripts/executable_render-jsonl.py` on main branch.
   
   - **`bd comment --body` failed** — `--body` is not a valid flag; bd comment accepts positional text, `--file <path>`, or `--stdin`. Fix: used `--file /tmp/bd-comment-progress.md`.
   
   - **`bd memories chezmoi worktree` rejected** (2 args) — `bd memories` accepts only 1 search arg. Fix: used single keyword `bd memories chezmoi`.
   
   - **First `bd create` failed with "no beads database found"** — cwd had been reset away from J121. Fix: explicit `cd ~/Source/J121 &&` prefix on bd commands.
   
   - **bd commands hang for minutes** — Dolt auto-commit + auto-sync adds significant latency. Fix: set long Bash timeouts (180000ms-300000ms), check output files after completion notifications arrive.
   
   - **J121 main push initially not completed** — first batched push command showed the header but no output for J121 main. Fix: explicit `cd ~/Source/J121 && git push origin main` succeeded.

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Transcript fidelity**: Acknowledged honest limitation (can't guarantee verbatim from my context buffer); used JSONL file at `~/.claude/projects/-home-hactar-Source-J121/bba28423-abec-4381-a6be-b17ff77e71ac.jsonl` as authoritative source, with rendered MD as lossy readable sibling. Discovered JSONL preserved a pre-edit version of user's Turn 3 prompt (had "Open question" before user reorganized as "First question / Second question").
   
   - **Memory sink redundancy**: Chose three sinks (J121 CLAUDE.md + bd remember + bead metadata) as "sufficient with reasonable certainty" — skipped auto-memory to avoid sprawl.
   
   - **Bead edge topology**: Researched dep types via `bd dep add --help`; used `discovered-from` (bd's formal name for "discovered-by") for research bead→ship bead edge; `relates-to` (symmetric) for sibling links. No blocking edges since Tier 1 ships independent of research outcome.
   
   - **Deferral mechanism**: bd has no "deferred" status; used `bd update --defer 2099-12-31` which hides from `bd ready` but stays visible via `bd list --deferred`.

6. All user messages:
   - "Let's continue with the autosession work. I have a few feature ideas: [5 bulleted design topics about --headless, ntfy.sh, git worktree, wrapper framework, and assorted open questions]... There's a lot to cover here--take your time and review each idea and question thoroughly..."
   - "Lots to respond to here. First, some housekeeping: we never closed the loop on approving deployment of your draft version of the autosession skill... My verdict: get it out of /tmp and into Chezmoi source control on a feature branch. Since the Chezmoi sync-state depends on the canonical Chezmoi worktree, please create a new worktree to use for the feature branch work. This worktree should become the default location of **all** Chezmoi feature development work not taking place directly on main. Record this preference... Next item: could you spool this entire conversation... Brief follow-up question for the previous ask: When I've asked you to 'spool transcripts' in the past, I've noticed that turns prior to the spool request sometimes get paraphrased on write. Is it possible for you to replay the conversation **exactly as it happened** up to this point..."
   - "put the script in a Chezmoi-managed 'global Claude scripts' directory at ~/.claude/scripts/. Make this change directly on `main` in the canonical Chezmoi repo and commit when done. Rationale: I want this script to be available to all Claude sessions in the future, and will likely use it to build a `/spool` skill (already scoped in beads)."
   - (task-notification about push completion — system message, not user intent)
   - "Follow-up to your Item 11 ('autosession-scoped beads sandbox'): Can Dolt be used to enable the following git-like workflow? [7 numbered steps]... Side question: are there good 'diff viewers' for Dolt DB states?... Follow-up to the follow-up: Does Dolt have a notion of 'worktrees' similar to git, and if so does it support the same multiple-worktree pattern we just implemented for Chezmoi feature branch work?"
   - "Nice. Create beads for the Tier 1 and Tier 3 versions you've proposed, with the Tier 3 marked as 'deferred' (or whatever the closest beads status analog is). Also create a 'research' bead to capture further investigation into the `dolt sql-server` + branch pinning sandboxing approach (per your 'I'm not sure how cleanly bd flips...' comment). Include a 'discovered-by' dep link from the current bead to the new research bead, and 'related-to' links between siblings where appropriate. Apart from that, use whatever edge topology you think fits. Finally, make sure to indicate your identity using the `bd --actor` flag."

7. Pending Tasks:
   - **Push `.beads` repo with bead creation changes** — bd operations (3 creates, 1 defer, 4 dep adds) modified `issues.jsonl`; needs commit + push to `AudensAurantius/J121-beads` per pre-authorized push rule in J121 CLAUDE.md. Earlier push already happened before bead creations; new changes are unpushed.

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before the summary request, I had just completed creating the three Dolt-sandbox-related beads and their edge topology per the user's Turn 6 instruction. Final verification via `bd show J121-9kp.2.17.5` and `bd list --label=area:session --status=open` confirmed:

This is directly implied by the pre-authorized push rule in J121 CLAUDE.md ("Before session end, ensure each [pre-authorized repo] has committed its changes and synced with its remote") and is the natural continuation of the just-completed bead creation work.

file.content:
Investigation bead: establish whether `dolt sql-server` with branch-pinning + scoped SQL grants can provide a Tier 2 beads sandbox — stronger than Tier 1 (permission-deny) without the duplicated-storage cost of Tier 3 (physical clone).

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation (sibling beads: Tier 1 active, Tier 3 deferred). The Tier 2 idea:

- Launch `dolt sql-server -B autosession/<slug> --port 3307` on the `.beads/` Dolt DB — server pinned to the autosession branch.
- Create a SQL user (`sandboxed`) with `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` on bd's data tables, but no grants on `dolt_branches`, `dolt_remotes`, or `DOLT_*()` system functions.
- Point bd at the server via whatever connection config bd supports, rather than embedded `.dolt/`.
- Coordinator has full bead mutation access but mechanically cannot switch branches or touch remote config.

The hinge question: **can bd flip from embedded-Dolt mode to remote-server mode cleanly?**

## Unknowns to resolve

1. **Backend flip.** Does bd expose connection config for a Dolt sql-server (host/port/user/password)? If not, is the patch to add it small (bd's Dolt layer is reportedly thin) or structural?
2. **System-function containment.** Do `DOLT_*()` functions respect SQL user grants? Specifically: can a user with `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` but no `EXECUTE` on `DOLT_CHECKOUT()` still query data from the pinned branch? Need to test — Dolt's system-function privilege model isn't identical to vanilla MySQL's.
3. **Branch pinning mechanics.** What exactly does `-B <branch>` do on `dolt sql-server` — lock the server to that branch, or just default new connections? Can a SQL user with `USE` privilege still switch branches via `CALL DOLT_CHECKOUT(...)` even on a pinned server?
4. **bd sync interaction.** bd's `issues.jsonl` export assumes embedded DB access. Does the export pathway still work against a sql-server backend, or would it need to be bypassed during sandbox sessions?
5. **Storage/startup cost.** sql-server per autosession is a daemon process; evaluate whether the startup latency and resource overhead make Tier 2 worth it compared to Tier 1's zero-process-overhead or Tier 3's duplicated-storage cost.

## Proposed probe

One-shot exploratory session — no production wiring:

1. Stand up a `dolt sql-server` on `.beads/` (throwaway scratch clone first; do not point at the canonical).
2. Create a scoped SQL user with the hypothesised grant set.
3. Attempt common bd operations against the server (manual SQL; bd-CLI flip may not be available yet).
4. Attempt branch-mutating operations as the scoped user to confirm they fail.
5. Document findings in a review doc: which unknowns are now resolved, which are still open, and whether Tier 2 is viable at all.

## Outputs

- `docs/reviews/dolt-sql-server-autosession-sandbox-<date>.md` — findings doc with yes/no/qualified verdict on Tier 2 viability.
- Updates to this bead: promote to implementation task if Tier 2 is viable; close as `--reason="not viable"` otherwise, with the Tier 3 sibling as the fallback.

## Why separate from the Tier 1 and Tier 3 beads

Tier 2 is a research question with a binary outcome — not an implementation task. Filing it as a peer bead (rather than a TODO inside Tier 1) prevents it from getting lost, gives it its own graph edges (`discovered-from` J121-9kp.2.17.1, `relates-to` the two sibling tiers), and makes the "I looked, here's what I found" output a first-class deliverable.

## Estimate

~2-4 hours for the probe + findings doc. Implementation work (if Tier 2 is viable) would be a separate bead filed off this one's findings.

Physical-isolation variant of Dolt-backed beads sandboxing. Deferred — ship the Tier 1 (permission-deny) sibling first, and only return here if Tier 1 demonstrates insufficient containment in practice.

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation. Three tiers were identified:

- **Tier 1** (sibling, active): branch + permission-deny. Cheap, policy-enforced. Fine until a coordinator demonstrates ability to circumvent.
- **Tier 2** (sibling, research): `dolt sql-server` + scoped SQL user. Depends on bd supporting remote-server backend; needs discovery.
- **Tier 3** (this bead): physical DB isolation via `dolt clone` to a session-scoped path.

Tier 3 is the strongest containment — the coordinator's `.beads/` points at a completely separate on-disk Dolt DB; nothing it does can reach the canonical DB because they share no filesystem path. Merge-back is a `dolt remote add canonical file://<canonical-path>` + `dolt push` from the sandbox, then `dolt merge autosession/<slug>` on the canonical side.

## Why deferred

Three reasons to defer:

1. **Tier 1 is probably enough.** The bead mutation policy already constrains coordinator behavior prescriptively. The incremental value of physical isolation over permission-deny is only realized if the coordinator proves to be an adversarial actor — which it isn't; it's a model following its brief.
2. **Sync complexity.** Two Dolt DBs must be kept coherent, and the `bd sync`/export workflow would need awareness of the sandbox path. Tier 1 uses the native branch mechanism and requires no sync plumbing.
3. **Storage cost.** `dolt clone` duplicates the full DB history per session. For a bead DB with thousands of issues and long history, that's non-trivial per-session overhead. Tier 1 is storage-free (branches are cheap).

## Scope if undeferred

1. `--dolt-sandbox=physical` variant on `scripts/scaffold-autosession.sh`.
2. At session start: `dolt clone file://$(readlink -f .beads) <session-dir>/.beads-sandbox`.
3. Session's bd commands target `--db <session-dir>/.beads-sandbox/<db-name>.db`.
4. At session shutdown: `dolt remote add canonical file://<canonical>` + `dolt push canonical autosession/<slug>` from the sandbox.
5. On canonical: `dolt fetch` + `dolt merge autosession/<slug>` during `/import-session` approval.

## Trigger conditions for undeferral

Return to this bead if any of:

- A session demonstrates ability to bypass Tier 1 permission-deny (e.g., coordinator finds a way to invoke dolt via a subprocess the deny doesn't cover).
- Multiple concurrent auto-sessions need non-interfering bead DBs (Tier 1 branches *do* interfere on `.beads/HEAD`; Tier 3 DBs don't share state).
- Audit/compliance posture requires demonstrable physical isolation rather than policy-based containment.

## Estimate if undeferred

~6-8 hours. Clone + sync plumbing, `--db` routing discipline across all bd invocations in the session, merge-back flow, and additional `/import-session` UX for the "discard the clone" path.

## Status

**Deferred indefinitely** — see `--defer` field on this bead. Surface via `bd list --deferred` when evaluating whether to undefer.

Ship the first and simplest tier of Dolt-backed auto-session beads sandboxing. Gives the user a pre-merge review window on whatever the coordinator did to bead state during a session, at low implementation cost.

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation (transcript at `~/Source/chezmoi-features/home/dot_claude/skills/auto-session/.transcripts/2026-04-22-autosession-design.jsonl`, turn-pair on the Dolt-as-sandbox follow-up). The existing bead mutation policy (`templates/beads/default.yaml`) constrains *how* the coordinator should mutate beads; Dolt branching adds the independent ability to *verify it did*. Those are orthogonal — the policy is prescriptive, the diff is descriptive.

## Scope

1. `--dolt-sandbox` flag on `scripts/scaffold-autosession.sh` (off by default).
2. When on, at session start:
   - `bd dolt commit -am "pre-autosession snapshot [<slug>]"` in `.beads/`
   - `dolt checkout -b autosession/<slug>` (bd uses the checked-out branch transparently)
3. Merge session-scoped permissions additions:
   - Deny `Bash(dolt:*)`, `Bash(bd dolt*)`, `Bash(bd sync*)`, `Bash(bd jira*)` — coordinator can mutate beads but not branches/remotes.
4. At session shutdown:
   - `bd dolt commit -am "end autosession [<slug>]"` on the autosession branch.
   - Leave the branch uncommitted-to-main for human review.
   - Emit a `dolt diff --json main autosession/<slug>` render to `<session-dir>/beads-diff.md` (small custom renderer; JSON → Markdown table grouped by table+row-action).
5. `/import-session` approval gate surfaces the diff alongside the existing exit-summary.
6. If approved: `dolt checkout main && dolt merge autosession/<slug>` inside `.beads/`; branch deleted. If rejected: branch retained for further review; `/import-session` offers a "discard branch" option that deletes it without merging.

## Out of scope (sibling beads)

- Physical isolation via `dolt clone` — sibling **Tier 3** bead, deferred until/unless Tier 1 proves insufficient.
- `dolt sql-server` + branch-pinned SQL user — sibling **research** bead; depends on bd supporting a remote-server backend, which is currently unknown.

## Verify

- Start a disposable `~/Source/_session-sandbox/` auto-session with `--dolt-sandbox`; confirm branch created, coordinator cannot run `dolt branch -D main`, beads mutations land on the autosession branch.
- Confirm `dolt diff main autosession/<slug>` at shutdown shows exactly the coordinator's changes and nothing else.
- Confirm `/import-session` can both merge and discard the branch.

## Pitfalls (already known)

- Permission-deny is tier-1 containment, not a hard sandbox. A coordinator willing to route through a shell script could still reach `dolt`. Policy-enforced, not mechanically enforced. Documented in the `--dolt-sandbox` flag's help text, not quietly glossed.
- `dolt diff` on a large schema can produce a lot of output. The renderer should group-by-table and cap rows-per-group before linking to the full JSON file.

## Estimate

~2-3 hours. Most of the work is the diff renderer; the branch + permission plumbing is thin.

S296 Redo Build System Tutorial — conceptual overview, use-cases, API design, worked examples, and minimal POSIX sh implementation (Apr 22, 8:05 PM)
**Investigated**: The redo build system as conceived by D.J. Bernstein and implemented by Avery Pennarun (apenwarr/redo): its dependency model, change-detection mechanism, .do file conventions, core commands, and comparison to Make/CMake/Bazel

**Learned**: Redo inverts Make's dependency model: instead of a central Makefile declaring dependencies, individual .do shell scripts call redo-ifchange at runtime to declare and satisfy their own dependencies. This makes the dependency graph emergent from actual builds rather than a maintained manifest.

    Redo uses content hashing (not mtime) for change detection — touching a file without changing it does nothing; reverting a file correctly triggers rebuilds. Build output is written atomically via a temp file ($3) moved to the target only on success.

    The .do file lookup order is: exact match (foo.bar.do), extension wildcard (default.bar.do), catch-all (default.do). Scripts receive $1=target, $2=base (stem without final extension), $3=temp output path.

    Core commands: redo (build targets), redo-ifchange (declare deps + ensure up to date), redo-always (mark as always-rebuild/phony), redo-ifcreated (rebuild if absent file appears).

    Key advantages over Make: no stale dependency declarations possible (deps declared at execution time), correct content-hash semantics, atomic output, shell-native build scripts. Key disadvantages: minimal ecosystem, POSIX-only, no IDE integration, no parallel build support in minimal implementations.

**Completed**: Delivered a complete tutorial covering:
    1. Conceptual overview of the runtime dependency declaration model
    2. Use-case analysis and comparison table vs Make/CMake/Bazel/Redo
    3. Full API reference (.do conventions, $1/$2/$3 args, all four commands, env vars)
    4. Five worked examples: single C file, C with automatic header deps, static site generator, protobuf codegen chain, phony targets
    5. A complete minimal redo implementation in ~150 lines of POSIX sh, dispatched by $0 symlink pattern, with content hashing, staleness checking, atomic output, and all four commands implemented
    6. Explicit list of what the minimal implementation omits vs production (parallel builds, cycle detection, out-of-tree builds, stdout capture)

**Next Steps**: No follow-on work indicated — this was a self-contained educational request with a complete response delivered.

[33m#S296[0m Redo Build System Tutorial — conceptual overview, use-cases, API design, worked examples, and minimal POSIX sh implementation (Apr 22, 8:05 PM)


[34mInvestigated:[0m The redo build system as conceived by D.J. Bernstein and implemented by Avery Pennarun (apenwarr/redo): its dependency model, change-detection mechanism, .do file conventions, core commands, and comparison to Make/CMake/Bazel

[33mLearned:[0m Redo inverts Make's dependency model: instead of a central Makefile declaring dependencies, individual .do shell scripts call redo-ifchange at runtime to declare and satisfy their own dependencies. This makes the dependency graph emergent from actual builds rather than a maintained manifest.

    Redo uses content hashing (not mtime) for change detection — touching a file without changing it does nothing; reverting a file correctly triggers rebuilds. Build output is written atomically via a temp file ($3) moved to the target only on success.

    The .do file lookup order is: exact match (foo.bar.do), extension wildcard (default.bar.do), catch-all (default.do). Scripts receive $1=target, $2=base (stem without final extension), $3=temp output path.

    Core commands: redo (build targets), redo-ifchange (declare deps + ensure up to date), redo-always (mark as always-rebuild/phony), redo-ifcreated (rebuild if absent file appears).

    Key advantages over Make: no stale dependency declarations possible (deps declared at execution time), correct content-hash semantics, atomic output, shell-native build scripts. Key disadvantages: minimal ecosystem, POSIX-only, no IDE integration, no parallel build support in minimal implementations.

[32mCompleted:[0m Delivered a complete tutorial covering:
    1. Conceptual overview of the runtime dependency declaration model
    2. Use-case analysis and comparison table vs Make/CMake/Bazel/Redo
    3. Full API reference (.do conventions, $1/$2/$3 args, all four commands, env vars)
    4. Five worked examples: single C file, C with automatic header deps, static site generator, protobuf codegen chain, phony targets
    5. A complete minimal redo implementation in ~150 lines of POSIX sh, dispatched by $0 symlink pattern, with content hashing, staleness checking, atomic output, and all four commands implemented
    6. Explicit list of what the minimal implementation omits vs production (parallel builds, cycle detection, out-of-tree builds, stdout capture)
